1.description: Predicted probabilities of time usage with the marginal effects of gender. Plotted in each of the panels above is the marginal effect of gender on the predicted probability of a time-use level (“much less,” “less,” “about the same,” “more,” and “much more”) from columns (1) to (6) of TableIII. In TableIII, the dependent variable for a column is a time-use variable—research, teaching, chores, childcare, leisure, or sleep. The estimation is an ordered logit. Independent variables are nonworking spouse, a cardinal child variable, a female indicator, and the interaction between the female indicator and the cardinal child variable. We take the marginal effect on the predicted probability of a response bucket by gender, at the mean of the other independent variables. The confidence bands shown are at the 95% level.

2.description: Predicted probabilities of time usage with the marginal effects of children. Plotted in each of the panels above is the marginal effect of the levels of the cardinal child variable on the predicted probability of a time-use level (“much less,” “less,” “about the same,” “more,” and “much more”) from columns (1) to (6) of TableIII. The levels of the child variable—the ages of children, if any—are given in the legend. In TableIII, the dependent variable for a column is a time use variable—research, teaching, chores, childcare, leisure, or sleep. The estimation is an ordered logit. Independent variables are nonworking spouse, a cardinal child variable, a female indicator, and the interaction between the female indicator and the cardinal child variable. We take the marginal effect on the predicted probability of a response bucket by the cardinal child variable, at the mean of the other independent variables. The confidence bands shown are at the 95% level.

3.description: Predicted probabilities of research productivity with marginal effects of gender and children. Plotted in each of the panels above is the marginal effect of gender (left panel) and children (right panel) on the predicted probability of theResearch_Productivityresponse taking one of the five choices (“much less,” “less,” “about the same,” “more,” and “much more”), from TableIV, column (1). The estimation is an ordered logit of responses to theResearch_Productivityquestion. Independent variables are nonworking spouse, a cardinal child variable, a female indicator, and the interaction between the female indicator and the cardinal child variable. In the left panel, we take the marginal effect on the predicted probability of a response bucket by gender, at the mean of the other independent variables. In the right panel, we take the marginal effect on the predicted probability of a response bucket by the cardinal child variable, at the mean of the other independent variables. The confidence bands shown are at the 95% level.

4.description: Marginal effects of time and production mechanisms on most negative research outcome. The figure presents the marginal effect of reporting the most extreme negative effect on research productivity (a score of 1 on the five-point Likert scale) associated with moving from “no change” (or a score of 3 on the five-point Likert scale) to “much more” (a score of 5) for a particular mechanism while all other covariates are assessed at their sample mean. Marginal effects are estimated for senior faculty, junior faculty, and PhD students using models (2), (4), and (6) of TableV, respectively. Panel A summarizes time allocation mechanisms (teaching, childcare, and chores). Panel B summarizes production mechanisms (concerns about feedback, isolation, and health).

5.description: Expected marginal effects of time and production mechanisms on most negative research outcome. The figure presents the expected marginal effect for a member of group (senior faculty, junior faculty, or PhD student) reporting the most extreme negative effect on research productivity that can be traced to a particular mechanism. The expected marginal effect is calculated as the marginal effect (reported in Figure4) multiplied by the probability that the group (senior faculty, junior faculty, or PhD student student) reports “much more” (a score of 5 on the five-point Likert scale) for a particular mechanism. Panel A summarizes time allocation mechanisms (teaching, childcare, and chores). Panel B summarizes production mechanisms (concerns about feedback, isolation, and health).

6.description: Predicted probabilities of research productivity and teaching with marginal effects of the financial condition of employer. Plotted in each of the panels above is the marginal effect of gender on the predicted probability ofResearch_Productivityresponse taking one of the five choices (“much less,” “less,” “about the same,” “more,” and “much more”), from TableVII, columns (1) and (3). The estimation is an ordered logit of responses to theResearch_Productivityquestion. Independent variables are nonworking spouse, a cardinal child variable, a female indicator, and the interaction between the female indicator and the cardinal child variable. In the left panel, we take the marginal effect on the predicted probability of a response bucket by gender, at the mean of the other independent variables. In the right panel, we take the marginal effect on the predicted probability of a response bucket by the cardinal child variable, at the mean of the other independent variables. The confidence bands shown are at the 95% level.
